subject: ap-chemistry
title: Reaction Rates, Stoichiometry & Acid-Base Buffers
stimulus:
A chemical reaction between maleic acid ($H_2C_4H_2O_4$) and sodium bicarbonate ($NaHCO_3$) occurs in the presence of water to produce carbon dioxide and sodium maleate ($Na_2C_4H_2O_4$), as represented by the following equation.

$H_2C_4H_2O_4$(aq) + 2$NaHCO_3$(aq) → 2$CO_2$(g) + 2$H_2O$(l) + $Na_2C_4H_2O_4$(aq)

parts.prompt:
A student combines equal masses of $H_2C_4H_2O_4$(s) chunks and $NaHCO_3$(s) chunks with sufficient water at 20.0°C. The student determines that 0.0114 mol of $CO_2$(g) is produced after the reaction goes to completion.

(i) Calculate the number of grams of $CO_2$(g) produced.

(ii) The $CO_2$(g) produced from the reaction at 20.0°C was collected and found to have a pressure of 1.25 atm. Calculate the volume of $CO_2$(g), in liters.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): mass = moles × molar mass = 0.0114 mol × 44.01 g/mol. Part (a)(ii): V = nRT/P using T = 293 K.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated mass of CO₂: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using molar mass of CO = 28 g/mol instead of CO₂ = 44.01 g/mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated volume using ideal gas law: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$T = 20.0°\text{C} + 273 = 293\text{ K}$ 
$V = \frac{nRT}{P} = \frac{(0.0114\text{ mol})(0.08206\text{ L·atm/mol·K})(293\text{ K})}{1.25\text{ atm}} = 0.219\text{ L}$

wrong_examples: Using T = 20.0 K instead of 293 K.
Dividing n by P without multiplying by RT.
common_traps: Always convert °C to K for ideal gas calculations.

parts.prompt:
The student performs a second experiment that is identical to the first except that the student grinds the chunks of $H_2C_4H_2O_4$(s) and $NaHCO_3$(s) into powder before combining the powder with water.

(i) What happens to the surface area of the reactants when the student grinds the chunks into powder?

(ii) The rate-determining step for the overall reaction is the dissolving of the solids. Would the time required for the dissolving of the solids in the second experiment be longer than, shorter than, or the same as the time required in the first experiment? Justify your answer based on the collisions between particles.

(iii) When the reaction is complete, will the volume of $CO_2$(g) at the end of the second experiment be greater than, less than, or equal to the volume at the end of the first experiment? Justify your answer.

The student conducts additional trials of the experiment and produces the following data table.

| Trial | Mass of $H_2C_4H_2O_4$ (g) | Mass of $NaHCO_3$ (g) | Moles of $CO_2$ Produced (mol) |
|-------|---------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| 3 | 1.543 | 1.251 | 0.01489 |
| 4 | 1.543 | 1.686 | 0.02007 |
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): surface area increases. Part (b)(ii): shorter — larger surface area means more frequent collisions between solid particles and water. Part (b)(iii): equal — same amounts of reactant produce same amount of product.

scoring_points.description: For the correct claim that surface area increases: 1 point

required_elements: The surface area of the solid reactants increases when ground into powder.
alternatives.correct_example: Grinding the chunks into powder breaks them into smaller particles, greatly increasing the total surface area exposed to the solvent.

wrong_examples: Surface area decreases — grinding into smaller pieces always increases total surface area.

scoring_points.description: For 'shorter than' with collision-based justification: 1 point

required_elements: Powdered reactants have greater surface area, leading to more frequent collisions between solid and water molecules, increasing the rate of dissolving.
alternatives.correct_example: Shorter than. Powdering the solids increases the surface area available for contact with water. More surface area means more collisions between solid particles and water molecules per unit time, increasing the dissolving rate and reducing the time required.

wrong_examples: Longer than — greater surface area speeds up dissolving, not slows it.
common_traps: Surface area affects rate (and therefore time), but not the amount of product formed.

scoring_points.description: For 'equal to' with stoichiometric justification: 1 point

required_elements: Both experiments start with the same masses (amounts) of reactants, so the same number of moles of CO₂ is produced.
alternatives.correct_example: Equal to. The same masses of reactants are used in both experiments. Since the amount of product depends on the amount of limiting reactant, not the rate of reaction, the final volume of CO₂ is equal in both experiments.

wrong_examples: Greater than or less than — changing particle size affects only the rate, not the stoichiometric yield.
common_traps: Rate and yield are independent; particle size affects rate but not equilibrium yield.

parts.prompt: Based on the student's data, identify the limiting reactant in trial 3. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept either a mole comparison or a rate-of-change argument. In trials 3 and 4: mass of H₂C₄H₂O₄ is constant but NaHCO₃ increases from trial 3 to 4, and CO₂ increases — so NaHCO₃ is limiting in trial 3.

scoring_points.description: For identifying NaHCO₃ as the limiting reactant with valid justification: 1 point

required_elements: When the mass of NaHCO₃ increases from trial 3 to trial 4 (while H₂C₄H₂O₄ is constant), more CO₂ is produced — indicating NaHCO₃ was not in excess in trial 3.
alternatives.correct_example: $NaHCO_3$ is the limiting reactant in trial 3. Comparing trials 3 and 4, the mass of $H_2C_4H_2O_4$ is the same (1.543 g) but the mass of $NaHCO_3$ increases from 1.251 g to 1.686 g. The amount of CO₂ also increases, showing that more NaHCO₃ produces more product — therefore NaHCO₃ was limiting in trial 3.

wrong_examples: H₂C₄H₂O₄ is limiting — if it were, increasing NaHCO₃ in trial 4 would not increase the yield.
common_traps: The limiting reactant is identified by determining which reactant, when increased, increases the product yield.

parts.prompt:
The reaction has a value of ΔS° greater than zero. Using particle-level reasoning, explain why the entropy
increases as the reaction progresses.

The student notices that the temperature of the reaction mixture decreases as the reaction takes place and correctly
determines that the reaction is endothermic.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any particle-level reasoning referencing production of gas (CO₂) from aqueous/solid reactants.

scoring_points.description: For a particle-level explanation of entropy increase: 1 point

required_elements: The reaction produces CO₂(g) from aqueous or solid reactants.
Gas molecules have far more positional freedom and microstates than aqueous or solid species, so entropy increases.
alternatives.correct_example: The reaction produces 2 moles of CO₂(g) for every mole of reaction. Gas molecules are highly dispersed compared to the aqueous reactants, having many more possible positions and orientations (microstates). This greater disorder results in a positive $\Delta S°$.

wrong_examples: Saying entropy increases because the products are more ordered — more order means less entropy.
common_traps: Gas production is the main driver of entropy increase in solution chemistry.

parts.prompt:
The student claims that the reaction is thermodynamically favorable at all temperatures because $\Delta S°_{rxn}$ > 0 and the reaction is endothermic. Do you agree or disagree with the student's claim? Justify your answer.

Next, the student investigates the acid-base behavior of maleic acid. The student notes that maleic acid is a diprotic acid. The two acid dissociation processes that occur are represented by the following equations.

$H_2C_4H_2O_4$ + $H_2O$ ⇌ $HC_4H_2O_4^-$ + $H_3O^+$     $K_{a1}$ = 1.5 × 10$^{-2}$
$HC_4H_2O_4^-$ + $H_2O$ ⇌ $C_4H_2O_4^{2-}$ + $H_3O^+$     $K_{a2}$ = 8.5 × 10$^{-7}$
parts.scoring_notes: Disagree. When ΔH > 0 and ΔS > 0: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS < 0 only when T > ΔH/ΔS. Below that temperature, ΔG > 0 (not favorable).

scoring_points.description: For 'disagree' with correct Gibbs free energy reasoning: 1 point

required_elements: $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$; with $\Delta H° > 0$ and $\Delta S° > 0$, $\Delta G° < 0$ only when $T > \Delta H°/\Delta S°$ (at sufficiently high temperature).
At low temperatures, $\Delta G° > 0$ and the reaction is not thermodynamically favorable.
alternatives.correct_example: Disagree. $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$. When $\Delta H° > 0$ (endothermic) and $\Delta S° > 0$, the sign of $\Delta G°$ depends on temperature: $\Delta G° < 0$ only when $T > \Delta H°/\Delta S°$. At temperatures below this threshold, $\Delta G° > 0$ and the reaction is not spontaneous. Therefore, the reaction is favorable only at sufficiently high temperatures, not at all temperatures.

wrong_examples: Agreeing — positive ΔS does not guarantee spontaneity at all temperatures when ΔH > 0.
common_traps: When both ΔH and ΔS are positive, the reaction is only favorable at high T. This is unlike ΔH < 0, ΔS > 0 (favorable at all T).

parts.prompt: Calculate the p$K_a$ value for the $HC_4H_2O_4^-$ ion.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated pKa2: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using Ka1 = 1.5×10⁻² — the question asks for the pKa of the HC₄H₂O₄⁻ ion, which corresponds to Ka2.
common_traps: HC₄H₂O₄⁻ is the conjugate acid of the second dissociation step; its Ka = Ka2 = 8.5×10⁻⁷.

parts.prompt: A buffer solution with a pH of 7.00 is prepared using $C_4H_2O_4^{2-}$ and $HC_4H_2O_4^-$. Calculate the ratio $\frac{[C_4H_2O_4^{2-}]}{[HC_4H_2O_4^-]}$ in this solution.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated ratio using Henderson–Hasselbalch: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using pKa1 = 1.70 instead of pKa2 = 6.07 — the buffer consists of the second acid/base pair.
Getting the ratio inverted (HA⁻/A²⁻ instead of A²⁻/HA⁻).
